speaker: interviewer
text: Bernard Presser, take number three. Can you continue telling me how you met-- how you got into Schindler's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I told you before. And on the-- when I met him-- when I met-- he met me in the gate and tells me-- Abraham was the Mr. Bankier who was, before the war, when they erected the factory, he was a silent partner to [INAUDIBLE] family, who was having one third of the interest in his factory. He-- he called Abraham. We-- we couldn't send Presser to the [? mail. ?] I will take him to the press room. And [INAUDIBLE] was the same story. He hid me in his office all the day. Because one Jew couldn't be without him. Because there was the different building. In the same factory, in the same [? department, ?] but was separately building. So next day, they ask the [INAUDIBLE] or the employment office, to send in a-- a few people with some kind of experience in some-- in tooling, or something of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then everybody tell him, oh I am. I am. I am, and the same. So I was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --one of the first.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they joined me so now there was 5 or 6 of us, or 10. I couldn't tell you how many. And Wianda, after a few weeks, a few days, while I was helping to-- the-- the-- that was, in very simple way, helping, building. Now, Wianda told me, Listen, I will let-- I will let you help set the press. And you will be the press setter. When I was the press setter, I had a seal for pressing to the press.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of the day, when I was with the foreman, I met Schindler. He was always very polite. He ask, how is my work? How is it going, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought-- and there was a matter of very short time, after that one, that Schindler come [BACKGROUND NOISE] to me--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just going to take this. Because the noise is--

speaker: interviewee
text: So yeah. --come to me and tell me, listen, the small press I have to arrange some work for-- for the Wehrmacht. Because they give me a-- a notice when I will not work for the [INAUDIBLE], they want to get the shipment out for-- for a mile. And he told me, we decided with Mr. [INAUDIBLE], German engineer, the top engineer, that this pressers and you would be in charge of it. I couldn't tell you anything about that one. I have no idea what I have to do. But I have to say yes. And this was-- that was the start of the-- that I-- I have been in charge of this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in end of '42. In-- in middle of-- or maybe first thing, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language did Schindler speak to you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I speak to him in Polish. And he-- he-- he tried to speak with me in Polish as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from this point-- what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: End of '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You started working in Schindler's factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. That was a first time when I have a contact with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you began to work for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you describe your work for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I told you, I was a foreman and press setter. They have to make some-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --something, what-- what the other people had been doing for-- for the full iron, we have to try to press the same thing with-- with only from the plate-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --who was-- were cutting and putting a-- by steps, by steps, from small round one to-- to deeper one and small in the-- in-- in diameter, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you making? What was the final product?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, late-- we-- we-- we find out very late, about it, what it was for. There was a case of-- have to be in the end of '32, or '33, that-- we haven't got-- they didn't accept it, Nazis, what we had been doing, very little. Because there was different dimensions, in the [? war, ?] of the product.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's another-- another [? story. ?] Schindler told me, listen, you have to try to [INAUDIBLE] the [? shape ?] [? holes. ?] The German who was overseeing the-- all stages of the production, to accept something. Because we haven't got nothing to [? work. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Till now, they-- they didn't accept it. They-- we sent 100 boxes of these-- these-- of these-- accepted one or two boxes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I approach him. And I told him, listen, I knew it would be not so strict for us. We will get some the-- additional bread for-- for Christmas.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me, listen. I will do it. But I have some restrictions for me in the way. I took this-- this one, the-- the polygon. This is the filled one. And I put explosive to it. And when the [? oil well ?] of this one exploded [? even, ?] I accepted. Even three quarters are accepted. But when only one side is going off, I couldn't accept that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, why? What-- I will-- what is the matter which is one or-- or the-- he told me, you know, they are parts of sea mines. So when we put inside and they exploded, but it will not explode everything. So they have to-- that's why. So, the first time I find out what it is we were-- we were doing. What-- what were you making, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: The casting for mines-- of sea mines. You know, the sea mines, what have the-- the magnetical something-- something on-- on top. And when-- when the ship touch it, they blow up the-- the mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were making that for the Wehrmacht--

speaker: interviewee
text: The casting. Only the casting. It was small-- small pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the German Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as I told you, that's hardly we have together. Because I believe a factory next to us in Brinnlitz had been doing the same, from the full-- but they had been losing a lot of-- a lot of-- of the-- of the iron. Because they have to-- to-- to cut it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How big was the factory? How many employees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the first-- before the-- the-- there was about 200 of us-- of us, in 1942. Or maybe over-- over 200. But when we have to make bigger arrangement for the Wehrmacht, we have been-- they started to build a-- a new-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] --very big hole for the big press who was coming. We have only one, two of them before. One who was [INAUDIBLE]. One was [INAUDIBLE]. The [INAUDIBLE] was a very strong one, hydraulic. And the [INAUDIBLE] was a-- a normal press, by-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How far from the ghetto was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three to four kilometers, or even more. I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No travel. We were few-- from November till end of-- end of January. We had been walking from ghetto to-- to [INAUDIBLE]. It was even closer. By January, or early in February, they transferred us to a labor camp in Plaszow. And we had been walking from Plaszow to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --to the factory. We have a shift of 12 hours, day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You said January, you went to Plaszow. January of which year?

speaker: interviewer
text: '42. OK. Were you still with your family in Plaszow? Who-- who went with you to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my wife was transferred in early January. And she was by-- I was by himself, and she was by herself. [LAUGHS] I was a-- in a good condition. I have a bed, living with Mr. and Mrs. Brown. She was cooking for me. So I was not bad. My wife was not-- not so good. Because she was working for Luftwaffe, at this-- and this, she was a-- cleaning lady in-- in a-- a offices over there. And she-- she lost her post as well, after they were-- after they were [? ousting ?] them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was doing some job in-- [DRINKS] --in the labor camp. I couldn't remember it. But when it had been over there, Mr. Bankier tried to influence Mr. Schindler, because he was was his right hand on the marketing mile.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Director ?] came for the people in-- in-- who are working for the-- in-- on the-- inside the factory, or next to the factory somewhere. The Schindler, I believe, started to-- to think about it. Well, because the Jewish worker had been doing quite-- do good job by this time. You have to remember that the Polish people had been working seven days and seven night. But they have to make a living as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they have to have one or two days away from the factory. Go into the country to bring some goods and selling, and so on. So Schindler realized that the Jewish worker in [? a mile ?] can do the job for him satisfactory job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he take as much as he can. And that he was not dependent on the Polish people to-- to be in factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did Schindler pay you for the work you were doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He pay to the German SS. I believe there were three marks for one day working.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he feed you meals?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we have to be feed by SS. But to tell you the true, I couldn't remember what I had for breakfast, what I have. But some kind of soup, they are giving us, when they are coming from the work. But in daytime, the Polish people have a separately, kitchen. And their soup was very good one. And sometimes happened that Schindler allowed us to have some soup in the-- the Polish. Besides that, he was-- oh, just to tell you how-- how friendly he was, you know, my mother was engaged in peeling the-- the potato in saltery, oldest-- oldest lady. Oldest. My wife-- mother was old by-- well, she was only 45 or 46. [LAUGHS] But she was peeling that-- the potato. And he was-- there was a colonel who was a very hard one. And they were cooking whatever they were. And Schindler was already coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: What they are cooking today for the-- for the dinner? That was the way he was treating us. So was your mother working for Schindler as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taking time. First I-- first I-- I went to-- to the-- to the place, what he erected for us. By this time, he knows the-- the-- the commander of the [INAUDIBLE] Goeth who was a devil-- devil, not a man. Completely was out in this-- in this thing. He was shooting people just as you would shoot-- shoot-- the-- the-- the dogs or something. All-- all for nothing. Now, well, we-- we hardly could-- could-- we-- you know, we know that was Himmler, when he shot something. You-- how that people who are living out of the ghetto can-- we can believe--

speaker: interviewer
text: When we couldn't believe that this-- he shot-- what for, he shot? Not for nothing. So it's very difficult to explain. So Schindler, I believe, find out what-- what type of-- of business he have to do. And he was going. And he was drinking, and make a party all of something.

speaker: interviewee
text: He get a-- the OK to-- to put the-- to make a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --a place for-- a camp for the worker of the-- first of all, it was only two men's barrack, and one for-- for girls. I was-- well, there was very few of us over there. Because there was very comfortable barracks. German-- he bought German barracks who was-- you have only to-- to put together-- to put together very quick, and were very clean. Very comfortable. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- and how did your mother come to work for Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the-- first of all, I was by-- by myself. And Rose, my mother, my sister, my future brother-in-law, and so on, they been-- still in-- in Plaszow. But if Schindler is want more workers, he have to apply to get-- to get more people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have been asking Schindler to bring my wife. First of all, she was coming. And later, it was-- it was progressing, I asked my sister, and my mother, and future-- future brother-in-law, who finish-- he was ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: He has finish the school of-- [? oh, it ?] was-- [? oh, it ?] was-- trading school in-- Jewish trading school as an-- goldsmith or something of this kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- later, Schindler make-- [? can't-- ?] to be sure that the people have been-- been happy working for him, he tried to-- to-- to bring the families of the people, that they have the satisfaction that they can be together-- To make you feel comfortable.

speaker: interviewee
text: --there. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the Polish co-workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a very good-- very good relation. As the-- for properties, the fishes rote from the top-- head. So when the Schindler was train-- treating us-- us well, they are treating us as well. Beside that one, I was very-- I, myself, was very popular with-- with them. Because I was doing-- I was foreman. I was doing very-- very-- very important job. And they know that I am. So I was very-- very friendly with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the barracks where you were living at the factory, were you able to stay with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or were the men's--

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife was living in a-- in a-- separately in a room. And I was living with-- in-- in men barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to spend much time together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, my wife was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] having migraine very often. And when she has migraines she couldn't do anything. She was completely sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ask the-- his foreman, send my wife to the barrack. Because she can't work. She don't have to go. So we-- it-- we knew that there was very big understanding to-- I couldn't express to you this one-- how people were quite friendly.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a rabbi in our place. He have to do some preparing to the [? eves ?] for-- for the [? topper ?] or whatever, see. Very-- very simple work. He have to put a few-- knock on it with a hammer. And it was all-- But he never do it. And [? all of a sudden, ?] I have a-- one example. A foreman from the-- Polish foreman, come to me, he tell me, Berek, take care of-- of my press. Because the bitch-- [LAUGHS] the son-of-the-bitch rabbi didn't do anything. And my people are working of piece work. I have to-- to take at least two hours to prepare everything for the nighttime and daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who said that to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The foreman. Polish. Mr. [INAUDIBLE], [? or ?] [? what it was. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of activities did you do when you weren't working?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a sense of community there, amongst the other workers? When you weren't at your job, what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I was occupied a whole day. One, there was something wrong with this machine. And the other one, I have to prepare or adjust it, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at times when you weren't working, how did you pass the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's very difficult to tell you. Because there was 12 hours working. When you come back, you have, probably, the soup [INAUDIBLE] in mind. You-- you have to have a showers over there. Cold water. And I was sitting in-- washing my-- not washing-- the-- I was always very-- I have some trouble with my feet. I-- they [? have been ?] tired. And I have to put in the hot water and keep it for a while. By this-- for-- for-- that's-- that's what's happened. I was sitting outside my barrack, and with feet in my-- in the water. All of a sudden, I-- I saw that something is running-- running from the-- from the air. There was-- later we find out there were-- there was a few days of-- or maybe the same day. Because I [? don't ?] [? see ?] [? it anymore. ?] There was plenty of barracks, other. And every-- everything in-- in a few minutes was in fire. I [INAUDIBLE] and run to-- to-- girls' barracks. To let-- to waken up and ask him to-- to go away, and run-- to run away. There was a-- a-- not pilot, the sitting-- [INAUDIBLE] man, who was with a machine gun. And he-- he falled in a-- in middle of a-- between our barracks. And it was completely burned out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the plane from?

speaker: interviewee
text: According to the gossip, I couldn't-- I couldn't be sure. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Allied forces had been bombarding Birkenau. There was a-- a synthetic factory of-- of petrol. And probably he was shot over there. And then dismembered over the Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the pilot from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, as we later find out, there was Australian one. There is now, a-- a plaque in the factory above the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Allied forces were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that it was. There was-- of course, I couldn't prove it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '93, about August, end of the August, something of this kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The day before, or a few days before, was the [INAUDIBLE]. And the-- the-- was-- Schindler was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he give a order from-- from-- I believe, from Goeth, That he have to give about 1,000 people or 800 people, away. They have to keep bombing this-- the people who-- who he need to-- evacuate the factory. Because there was a Russian offensive. And they stay in some-- they [INAUDIBLE], or whatever. And the German had been in very big distress. And they're running away, prepare for themselves to-- to [? catch ?] what they [? have kept, ?] and so on. And that was a lucky thing. Because all the barracks were empty now. It was burned off completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the plane crashed, there was nobody injured?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody on the ground?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did those 1,000 people go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say, Goeth, the commandant of Plaszow--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --asked for 1,000 people, where were they sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- they-- they-- they was-- as I-- as I find later, they went to Austria, to the-- one of the convents. But I-- I couldn't tell you the [? very-- ?] and next to the [? green ?] [? grass. ?] Very, very-- there is a little [? kidding ?] [? place, ?] not by [? kidding, ?] but you have a stone and-- to take from one place, 100 step out, 120 step [? out. ?] And one back. And we are not running. We have been hitting. And they hit you on the [? head ?] [? and ?] [INAUDIBLE]. Now, that one I-- I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- how-- what-- how-- oh, the-- Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were left at Schindler's factory when these 1,000--

speaker: interviewee
text: About 1,200-- 300 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still at the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. About that, after-- before the [? all ?] [INAUDIBLE], we have to take about 800 and something of these people. They had been sent to the Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop and change the tape. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--
